coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots efficiently promote vascularization for enhanced repairing of orthopedic diseases with diabetic mellitus based on nanocatalytic medicine

coredata.dc:description: Insufficient angiogenesis, a prominent characteristic of diabetic mellitus, significantly affected the reparative capacity of orthopedic diseases. Imbalanced redox caused by excessive reactive oxygen species is one of the crucial factors in weakening vascularization. Conditions such as implant osseointegration failure and delayed wound healing in orthopedic diseases are significantly influenced by the vascularization deficits in diabetic mellitus. Promoting angiogenesis by modulating endothelial function is considered as an effective therapy for diabetes, yet current efforts remain insufficient. Nanocatalytic medicine has emerged as promising candidates for therapy. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with preferable catalytic effect by mimicking both superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT)-like activity were designed and synthesized using anhydrous citric acid and ethane diamine. CDs effectively scavenged superoxide anion (O2 •−) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with excellent biocompatibility. Meanwhile, CDs promoted the proliferation, migration, and angiogenesis capacity of human umbilical vein endothelial cells with enhanced SOD and CAT activity in simulated oxidative stress of diabetes. Notably, CDs promoted vascularization by activate PI3K-AKT signaling pathway in impaired endothelial cells, which was demonstrated through transcriptome sequencing, western blot, and reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction. The therapeutic effect of CDs based on promoting vascularization was found in orthopedic disease models of subcutaneous implant and wound healing of diabetic rat. These findings highlight a promising clinical application for CDs as a nanocatalytic medicine in enhancing vascularization for diabetic mellitus.

(D) DPPH• scavenging efficiency of CDs compared with that of AA. (E) O2 •− scavenging rate of CDs. (F) H2O2 scavenging rate of CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbon dots efficiently promote vascularization for enhanced repairing of orthopedic diseases with diabetic mellitus based on nanocatalytic medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Insufficient angiogenesis, a prominent characteristic of diabetic mellitus, significantly affected the reparative capacity of orthopedic diseases. Imbalanced redox caused by excessive reactive oxygen species is one of the crucial factors in weakening vascularization. Conditions such as implant osseointegration failure and delayed wound healing in orthopedic diseases are significantly influenced by the vascularization deficits in diabetic mellitus. Promoting angiogenesis by modulating endothelial function is considered as an effective therapy for diabetes, yet current efforts remain insufficient. Nanocatalytic medicine has emerged as promising candidates for therapy. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with preferable catalytic effect by mimicking both superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT)-like activity were designed and synthesized using anhydrous citric acid and ethane diamine. CDs effectively scavenged superoxide anion (O2 •−) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) with excellent biocompatibility. Meanwhile, CDs promoted the proliferation, migration, and angiogenesis capacity of human umbilical vein endothelial cells with enhanced SOD and CAT activity in simulated oxidative stress of diabetes. Notably, CDs promoted vascularization by activate PI3K-AKT signaling pathway in impaired endothelial cells, which was demonstrated through transcriptome sequencing, western blot, and reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction. The therapeutic effect of CDs based on promoting vascularization was found in orthopedic disease models of subcutaneous implant and wound healing of diabetic rat. These findings highlight a promising clinical application for CDs as a nanocatalytic medicine in enhancing vascularization for diabetic mellitus.

Carbon dots prepared from controllably hydrothermal strategy, acting as nanocatalytic medicine, exhibit both SOD-like and CAT-like activities for promoting angiogenesis with clarified molecular mechanism to effectively intervene diabetes-associated orthopedic diseases.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a chronic disease characterized by hyperglycemia or an increased level of blood glucose. The prevalence of diabetes is steadily rising worldwide, primarily attributed to the growing obesity rates and aging demographic. It is estimated that the global prevalence of diabetes will reach 10.2% by 2030 [1,2]. Difficulty in neovascularization is a common complication of diabetes, arising from various abnormal physiological conditions [3]. When combined with diabetes, orthopedic diseases like bone defects pose considerable challenges. Conventional approaches to bone defect repair involve autogenous and allogeneic bone grafts, along with internal fixation. However, in diabetic individuals, the local microenvironment hinders angiogenesis around bones and implants, leading to inadequate nutritional support for bone tissue repair and thus, poor osseointegration [4,5]. Despite extensive exploration of medical interventions for orthopedic patients with diabetes, therapeutic efficacy remains elusive due to the compromised angiogenic abilities. Consequently, difficulties persist in implant integration and non-healing tissue persist [6–8]. Therefore, there is a pressing need for the development of drugs with a clarified mechanism that promotes vascularization to facilitating the rehabilitation of orthopedic patients with diabetes (see Scheme 1 ).

Emerging evidence has revealed that diabetic patients experience an imbalance of oxidation and antioxidants with excessive production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [9,10], which contain the components of superoxide anion (O2 •−), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radical (.OH), and singlet oxygen (1O2). Prolonged periods of elevated blood glucose level divert glucose metabolism towards alternative pathways, generating more ROS through mechanism such as methylglyoxal/glycation, enediol formation, activation of diacyl-glycerol/protein kinase C, hexosamine metabolism, and sorbitol metabolism [11]. In orthopedic patients with diabetes, excessive ROS levels contribute to osteoblast dysfunction and cell apoptosis, exacerbating impaired bone tissue repair and integration [12,13]. In addition, overproduction of ROS disrupts endothelial dysfunction by oxidizing low-density lipoprotein (LDL) and deactivating nitric oxide (NO). They also activate the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) and phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase (PI3K)/protein kinase B (AKT)/(PI3K/AKT) signaling pathway, leading to upregulation of redox transcriptional factors and genes, culminating in endothelial cell dysfunction [14,15]. The design of strategies to promote angiogenesis by maintaining redox homeostasis of local lesions in diabetic patients has broad application prospects for achieving satisfactory repairing of orthopedic diseases [16,17]. There is an urgent need to design a new biological materials or drug systems to tackle these problems [18,19].

In contrast to the potential drawbacks of small molecular drugs, such as limited absorption and low bioavailability in the body [18,19], nanoparticles-based medications have emerged as valuable therapeutic tools and drug delivery systems, owing to their customizable size and adaptability [20]. The combination of nanotechnology with functional materials has made remarkable progress in treating a spectrum of diseases [21–23]. Nanocatalytic drugs have demonstrated heightened antioxidative stability and exceptional resilience in the challenging microenvironments of diseases [24,25]. Carbon dots, nanostructures derived from carbon with a zero-dimensional configuration, exhibit excellent biocompatibility and adjustable biological capabilities [26–28]. Through manipulation of preparation materials, reaction conditions, and purification techniques, carbon dots with distinct structural variations and functional modifications can be obtained for the diagnosis and treatment of diverse diseases [29,30]. Previous studies have successfully synthesized positively charged carbon dots [31], quaternized carbon quantum dots [32], and natural biomass-derived carbon dots with antibacterial activity against drug-resistant bacteria using controllable raw materials [33]. Moreover, the field of biomedical applications for carbon dots is currently undergoing significant advancements [34–36]. While some studies have investigated the potential medical applications of carbon dots [37,38], there is a lack of comprehensive understanding regarding the performance and molecular mechanism underlying the promotion of vascularization by restoring normal endothelial function for clinical treatments in carbon dots and other forms of nanomedicine.

In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized as nanocatalytic medicine through a one-step hydrothermal process using anhydrous citric acid and ethane diamine. The morphology and composition of the CDs were characterized, followed by a rigorous assessment of their biological enzymatic activity in terms of SOD and CAT. Subsequently, the biocompatibility and angiogenic potential were evaluated in vitro using human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs). Transcriptome sequencing, western blot, and reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) were systematically used to clarify with the molecular mechanism of the promoting angiogenesis of CDs in H2O2-damaged HUVECs, which is a well-established in vitro model simulating diabetes [38,39]. The results suggested that CDs enhanced angiogenesis in damaged HUVECs through activation of the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway. Moreover, in vivo diabetes-associated orthopedic diseases of subcutaneous implant and wound healing were established as the model disease in rat. The in vivo results further confirmed that the CDs acted as nanocatalytic medicine, effectively promote angiogenesis for the repair of orthopedic diseases associated with diabetes.

CDs were prepared using one-step hydrothermal method. In brief, 1.0 g anhydrous citric acid was dissolved in 19 mL ultrapure water using ultrasound to obtain a homogeneous solution. Then 1 mL ethane diamine was added into solution under stirring at room temperature for 10 min. The reacted solution was transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the solution was filtered using 0.22 μm filter membrane, followed by further dialysis (500-1000 Da) to remove the unreacted precursors. The resulting CDs were stored at 4 °C after lyophilization.

Briefly, 7 mM ABTS and 2.45 mM potassium persulfate were mixed and incubated at 37 °C in the dark for more than 16 h to prepare the ABTS working solution. The working solution of ABTS was diluted 50 times with PBS, and then mixed with different concentrations of CDs solution (0-200 μg/mL). After 10 min of reaction under light, the absorbance at 734 nm was measured by UV–vis spectroscopy (UV-2450 (Shimadzu, Japan)). Correspondingly, ascorbic acid (AA) was investigated as the standard control. The ABTS scavenging abilities were calculated as follows:

Where A0 is the absorbance of a standard without CDs, and AS is the absorbance after the reaction with the CDs, respectively.

Using AA as the standard control, DPPH• ethanol solution (200 μM) was mixed with CDs of different concentrations (0-200 μg/mL). The reaction was performed for 1 h at room temperature in the dark. The specific absorbance of DPPH• at 526 nm was recorded by UV–vis spectroscopy. The DPPH• scavenging abilities were calculated as follows:

Where D0 is the absorbance of a standard without CDs, and DS is the absorbance after the reaction with the CDs, respectively.

The O2 •− scavenging capacity of different concentrations of CDs (0-200 μg/mL) were assessed used superoxide anion assay kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China) according to the manufacturer's instructions. It mimicked the reaction system of xanthine (X) and xanthine oxidase (XO) in the body which produced O2 •−. When the tested sample contained O2 •− radical inhibitor, its absorbance would be reduced. By using AA as the standard, the influence ability of the tested sample on O2 •− radical could be calculated.

Different concentrations of CDs (0-200 μg/mL) were incubated with 250 μM H2O2 at 37 °C for 2 h in dark. After reaction, the residual H2O2 was evaluated by hydrogen peroxide detection kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China) following the manufacturer's instructions, and CDs' H2O2-scavenging activity was assessed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Superoxide anion (O2 •−) was generated by the standard X/XO system. Firstly, PBS containing 1 mM diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid dianhydride (DTPA) and 0.4 mM X was prepared as solution A, and PBS containing 0.1 U/mL XO was prepared as solution B. Then, after incubating 135 μL of solution A, 25 μL of PBS or 40 μg/mL of CDs, and 20 μL of solution B for 1 min, 20 μL of BMPO (25 mM) was added for ESR measurement.
The 100 μL of 250 μM H2O2 was incubated with 100 μL of PBS or 40 μg/mL of.
CDs for 2 h in dark. Then, 10 μL of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) (0.2 M) was added for ESR measurement.

The HUVECs were obtained from National Collection of Authenticated Cell Cultures in China. The HUVECs were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), 100 μg/mL streptomycin, and 100 U/mL penicillin at 37 °C in an incubator supplied with 5% CO2. For suppression and activation of the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway, HUVECs were exposed to 30 μM LY294002 (the inhibitor of PI3K signaling; MCE) or 30 μM 740 Y-P (the activator of PI3K signaling; MCE) at the same time of CDs treatment.

The HUVECs were seeded in a 96-well plate at 6000 cells/well, and then cultured with different concentrations of H2O2 (0, 1, 10, 100, 300, 500, 700 and 1000 μM). After incubation for 24 h, the cytotoxicity was evaluated by CCK-8 method. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured by the microplate reader (MultiskanGO (Thermoscientific, USA)), and cell viability was calculated as following:
(3)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
O D
s
−
O D
b
O D
c
−
O D
b
× 100
%
Where ODs is the absorbance of cells incubated with CDs, ODc is the absorbance of cultured cells without CDs, and ODb is the absorbance of single medium, respectively. Also, the cell viability of different concentrations of CDs (0-50 μg/mL) in normal and H2O2-induced oxidative stress injury in HUVECs were investigated.

The Calcein/PI double staining kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, China) was used to further assessed the viability of HUVECs seeded on CDs for 24 h under oxidative stress or not. The images were captured using Leica DMIRB inverted fluorescence microscope (Leica, USA).

In brief, HUVECs were seeded in 24-well plate at 1.0 × 105 cells/well with or without DMEM containing 500 μM H2O2 to simulate the presence of oxidative stress. When the cells reached 90% confluence, a scratch was made by 10 μL pipette tip followed by PBS washed three times. Different concentrations of CDs (5, 20, 40 μg/mL) were incubated with HUVECs for 24 h, and images of each well were recorded by inverted fluorescence microscope. The area of each wound at determined time was measured using ImageJ software (NIH, Bethesda, MD, USA), and the wound closure rate was calculated as following:
(4)
Wound
closure
rate =
(
W 0
−
W s
)
W 0
× 100
%
Where W0 is the area of wound which cells incubated with CDs for 0 h, and Ws is the area of wound which cells incubated with CDs for determined hours, respectively.

HUVECs were seeded in 6-well plates (1.5 × 105 cell/well) with or without DMEM containing 500 μM H2O2 to simulate the presence of oxidative stress and cultured for 24 h. Then the medium was changed with 2 mL fresh medium containing varing concentrations of CDs (5, 20, 40 μg/mL). After cultured for 24 h, cells were harvested and washed twice with PBS. For cell apoptosis assay, cells were stained by Annexin V-FITC cell apoptosis kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, China) according to manufacturer's instructions. Finally, the stained cells were analyzed by Flow Cytometer (BD Bioscience, USA). One Step TdT-mediated dUTP Nick-End Labeling (TUNEL) Apoptosis Assay Kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, China) was applied to further determine cell apoptosis. Pretreated cells in 96-well plates were washed twice with PBS, incubated with 50 μL TUNEL testing solution at 37 °C for 1 h in the dark, and then washed with PBS for three times. After the membrane was sealed with anti-fluorescence quenching solution, the cells were observed at an excitation wavelength of 550 nm and the emission wavelength of 570 nm using an inverted fluorescence microscope. The proportion of TUNEL positive cells were calculated by ImageJ software.

The intracellular ROS levels were measured using the 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) staining assay. HUVECs were seeded in 96-well plates at 6000 cells/well, and DMEM containing 500 μM H2O2 used to induce oxidative stress. After fully spread, different concentrations of CDs (5, 20, 40 μg/mL) incubated with HUVECs for 24 h. Then the medium was replaced by serum-free DMEM containing 10 μM DCFH-DA for 30 min in the dark. The images of each well were detected by inverted fluorescence microscope, and average fluorescence intensity was measured by ImageJ software. Similar to the previous intervention, HUVECs were incubated with CDs (5, 20, 40 μg/mL) under 500 μM H2O2. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity, catalase (CAT) activity and malondialdehyde (MDA) level were assessed by the corresponding detection kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, China).

The mitochondrial function was assessed by a mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit with JC-1 (Beyotime Biotechnology, China) according to the manufacturer's instructions. In brief, after the intervention mentioned above, HUVECs were washed with PBS and treated with JC-1 at 37 °C for 20 min in dark conditions. Then the results were recorded by inverted fluorescence microscope, and average fluorescence intensity was calculated by ImageJ.

The angiogenesis potential of CDs was evaluated by conducting a tube formation assay. Briefly, in 96-well culture plates, 60 μL of Matrigel (BD Bioscience, USA) per well was added and allowed to solidify at 37 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, HUVECs were seeded at a density of 6000 cells/well and treated with various concentrations of CDs (5, 20, 40 μg/mL). After 6 h of incubation under oxidative stress conditions mimicked by 500 μM H2O2, the formation of tubes (three fields per group) was visualized and imaged using inverted fluorescence microscope. The branch points and the length of tubes were analyzed using ImageJ software.

In a 24-well plate, HUVECs (1.0 × 105 cells/well) were cocultured with different concentrations CDs (5, 20, 40 μg/mL) under 500 μM H2O2 for 24 h. After that, the concentration of VEGF-A in the culture medium was determined with an ELISA kit (Elabscience Biotechnology Co., China) according to the manufacture's introduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA extraction, cDNA library construction, and transcriptome sequencing
ce:section.ce:para: RNA extraction, cDNA library construction and transcriptome sequencing were implemented by 10K Genomics (Shanghai, China). In brief, oxidized HUVECs cultured with 40 μg/mL CDs for 24 h served as the treatment group, while oxidized HUVECs without CDs intervention served as the control group. Then total RNA of cell was extracted using Trizol reagent (Invitrogen, USA), dissolved in ultrapure water, and quantified for quality control using Agilent Bioan/alyzer 2100 system (Agilent Technologies, CA). Qualified RNA samples (concentration was greater than 200 ng/μL, RIN value was greater than 7.2, 28S:18S value was greater than 1.0) were used for sequencing library buliding. RNA library preparation kit was used to for library preparation. Random primers and corresponding synthesis kit was used to synthesize the double stranded cDNA. According to the library construction scheme, the synthesized cDNA double strand was analyzed and modified, and finally the cDNA library was established. Qualified samples were sequenced using Illumina HiSeq X Ten sequencing platform. Differentially Expressed Genes (DEGs) were statistically analyzed by different databases including Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG). Three parallel samples were collected for each group.

The cells were scratched and collected in RIPA buffer (Santa Cruz Biotech, TX, US). The total protein was quantified with BCA assay (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, US), and 30 μg of protein lysate was loaded and separated with 8.5–12.5% SDS-PAGE gel. After transferring to polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane, the membranes were blocked using 5% bovine serum albumin (BSA) and incubated with primary antibodies overnight at 4 °C. Suitable HRP-conjugated secondary antibodies (Cell Signaling Technology, MA, US) were used, and SuperSignal West Femto Maximum Sensitivity Substrate (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, US) was used to detect the bands. β-actin was as an equal loading control. Primary antibodies and dilutions used were as follows: p-PI3K (1:1000; Zen-Bioscience, China); PI3K (1:1000; Zen-Bioscience, China); p-AKT (1:1000; Cell Signaling Technology, MA, US); AKT (1:1000; Cell Signaling Technology, MA, US); VEGFA (1:1000; Elabscience Biotechnology, China); Nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) (1:1000; Proteintech, PA, US); and β-actin (1:5000; Proteintech, PA, US)

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA extraction and reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In the 96-well plates, HUVECs (6000 cells/well) were cultured with different concentrations of CDs (5, 20, 40 μg/mL) for 24 h. Then the total RNA was isolated from the cells using Trizol reagent (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, USA), according to the manufacturer's instructions. The following genes were measured with RT-PCR: SOD2 (superoxide dismutase 2), CAT (catalase), and VEGFA (vascular endothelial growth factor A). The standard method for the aforementioned genes were applied by normalizing to GAPDH and the calculating using the 2−ΔΔCt method. RT-PCR was performed in a Stratagene Mx3000P system (Agilent Technologies). The primer sequence for each gene listed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental animals of diabetes-associated orthopedic diseases
ce:section.ce:para: Six-month-old male Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats weighing 270-290 g (Fujian Medical University Laboratory Animal Center) were used in all experiments. They were kept on a 12-h:12-h light-dark cycle with free access to food and water. The animal experiment procedures were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Fujian Medical University (FJMU IACUC 2023-0029).

After a week of adaptive feeding, SD rats were given a single intraperitoneal injection of streptozotocin (STZ) (50 mg/kg) in 0.1 M citrate buffer (pH 4.5). 48 h after the injection, T2DM SD rats were selected from those whose fasting random blood glucose levels exceed 16.7 mmol/L. Twelve T2DM SD rats were randomly divided into four groups: positive control (PC), CP5, CP20, and CP40. Polyetheretherketone (PEEK) (10 mm (Φ) × 2 mm (h)) was used as the implantation loaded with PBS, 5, 20, and 40 μg/mL CDs by immersion coating method for the four groups, respectively. After animals anesthetized, the hair on the back of rats was removed. A longitudinal incision was made in the middle of the back to place the pure PEEK, CP5, CP20, and CP40 in the subcutaneous tissue on both sides of the rats and the skin was sutured. Three normal SD rats with pure PEEK implantation served as the negative control (NC).

Twenty-four STZ induced T2DM SD rats were anesthetized, hair on the dorsal surface of the skin was shaved, and a full-thickness skin wound with a diameter of 2 cm was created on each rat's back. Then, these rats were divided into four groups (each group with six rats) randomly: positive control (T2DM SD rats; wound treated with topical application of 1 mL normal saline once every two days), C5 (T2DM SD rats; wound treated with topical application of 1 mL 5 μg/mL CDs once every two days), C20 (T2DM SD rats; wound treated with topical application of 1 mL 20 μg/mL CDs once every two days), and C40 (T2DM SD rats; wound treated with topical application of 1 mL 40 μg/mL CDs once every two days). Six normal SD rats with wound topical injection with normal saline were set as negative control group. The wounds were photographed and measured using ImageJ software on days 0, 3, 7, 10, and 14 following the procedure. The percentage of wound healed area was calculated as following:
(5)
The
percentage
of
wound
healed
area =
(
W 0
−
W s
)
W 0
× 100
%
Where W0 is the area of wound on days 0, and Ws is the area of wound for determined days, respectively.

Collected full-thickness tissue samples of subcutaneous angiogenesis and wound healing model were grinded by TGrinder H24R Tissue Grinder (TianGen, China). Then, the SOD activity, CAT activity and MDA level were assessed by the corresponding detection kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, China) following the manufacturer's instruction.

The full-thickness tissue samples from the subcutaneous angiogenesis and wound healing model were collected on determined days. The sections of the tissue were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson's trichrome following the manufacturers' protocol (Servicebio technology Co., Ltd., Wuhan, China) to visualize the reepithelialization/granulation tissue formation and collagen deposition, respectively. Meanwhile, the anti-CD31 antibody (1:300; Product # GB12063; Servicebio), the anti-vWF antibody (1:200; Product # GB11020; Servicebio), the anti-Collagen I antibody (1:300; Product # GB114197), and the anti-Nrf2 antibody (1:200; Product # GB113808; Servicebio) IHC were performed. Goat anti-rabbit IgG H&L (HRP) (1:200; Product # GB23302; Servicebio) was used as a secondary antibody.

The optical properties, morphology, and chemical structure of CDs were comprehensively characterized. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, the fluorescence intensities of the CDs exhibited a peak and then fluctuated across different excitation wavelengths (300-400 nm), exhibiting excitation-independent emission property. In addition, the CDs displayed maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 350 nm and 441 nm, respectively. The absorption spectrum of the CDs revealed the absorbance peaks at 238 nm and 350 nm (Fig. 1A), attributed to the π-π* transition of sp2 hybridization of the CC bond and n-π* transition of CO [39,40], respectively. The absorbance of CDs at 350 nm was corresponding to the optimal excitation wavelength. TEM result showed that CDs (Fig. 1B) were a regular monodisperse spherical structure with a particle size distribution between 1.3 and 4.4 nm and an average diameter of 2.8 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.28 nm was clarified through high resolution TEM (HRTEM), suggesting the diffraction characteristics of graphite carbon [41,42].

The chemical structure of CDs was evaluated. The XPS full-wavelength spectrum of CDs had three typical peaks of C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (399.9 eV) and O1s (530.9 eV), with corresponding element percentages of 66.89%, 15.26% and 17.85%, respectively (Fig. 1C). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 1D) generated three fitting bands, which were assigned to CC/C-C (284.5 eV), C-O/C-N (285.6 eV) and -CO (287.6 eV) [43]. Among them, the structure of CC/C-C (the sp2 hybrid carbon network structure) accounts for 21.59% of the total element content of CDs. The two fitted bands in the high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s (Fig. 1E) were corresponded to 531 eV (-COOH) and 532 eV (C-OH/C-O-C), accounting for 14.16% and 3.69% of the total element content of CDs, respectively [44]. Furthermore, the binding energies of 399.9 eV (N-H, 12.69%) and 401.3 eV (C-N, 2.57%) were obtained by fitting the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s (Fig. 1F) [45], indicating that CDs were successfully doped with electro-rich N element and the surface of CDs was rich in electron-donating functional groups, such as amino group. In addition, the FTIR spectrum showed (Fig. 1G) the absorption peaks of CA and CDs at 3431 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H and CO bonds, while the absorption peaks of EDA and CDs at 1556 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and C-N bonds, respectively. The chemical structure of CDs was further characterized using NMR. The 1H NMR spectrum of CDs exhibited a signal of 1-2 ppm corresponding to aliphatic hydrogens (Fig. 1H and I). Those peaks between 2-4 ppm could be attributed to specific functional groups of CH-N, O-H, and N-H. The large resonance at 4.70 ppm was corresponding to the O-H group, which may be from D2O. The peak signals between 20 and 100 ppm in the 13C NMR spectrum were ascribed to C-C, C-N, and C-O sp3 hybridized aliphatic C atoms. While the peaks between 100 and 140 ppm were assigned to CN sp2 hybridized C atoms [23]. The atoms of the carboxy and amide groups were detected in the range of 160–180 ppm of 13C NMR spectrum. NMR spectra prove that CDs have a hybrid structure including sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms. FTIR results was consistent with XPS and NMR characterization, which confirmed the existence of the functional groups of -NH2, -OH and -COOH in CDs [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mimicking both superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT)-like activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 A, the presence of CDs led to a noticeable reduction in absorbance and discoloration of the blue-colored ABTS•+ radicals. The scavenging efficiency of ABTS•+ radicals increased proportionally with rising concentrations of CDs. The ABTS•+radicals scavenging efficiency reached almost 100% when the CDs’ concentration up to 15 μg/mL compared to AA at the concentration of 8 μg/mL. The EC50 of CDs and AA was approximately 5.84 μg/mL and 3.21 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. 2B). The stability of the antioxidant activity of CDs was confirmed through similar results obtained after one and three months of storage in ultrapure water (Fig. S1). After one month, the EC50 of CDs and AA was approximately 8.08 μg/mL and 3.33 μg/mL, respectively. After three months, the EC50 of CDs and AA was approximately 9.18 μg/mL and 4.58 μg/mL, respectively. In the DPPH• radicals scavenging assay, the scavenging efficiency of CDs followed a similar trend to that of AA. Both CDs and AA exhibited increased DPPH• scavenging efficiency with increasing concentrations, eventually stabilizing at around 80% scavenging (Fig. 2C). The EC50 of CDs and AA in the DPPH• radicals scavenging assay were approximately 12.56 μg/mL and 7.88 μg/mL (Fig. 2D), respectively. These results highlight the excellent scavenging activity of CDs against ROS.
Subsequently, the scavenging activity of CDs towards superoxide anion (O2 •−) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was evaluated. According to the superoxide anion assay kit.

(Fig. 2E), a remarkably low concentration of CDs (12 μg/mL) was able to decompose approximately 50% of O2 •−, and the inhibition of O2 •− could exceed 75% at the concentration of 25 μg/mL CDs, indicating the high O2 •− scavenging activity of CDs. Moreover, CDs exhibited a concentration-dependent scavenging effect on H2O2, with more than 50% inhibition achieved at 80 μg/mL CDs assessed by hydrogen peroxide detection kit (Fig. 2F). Additionally, the free radical scavenging capacity of CDs was evaluated by ESR. As shown in Fig. S2, the radical intensity of O2 •− and H2O2 were reduced with the treatment of CDs. Such results were consisted with the outcomes of assay kit, indicating preferable scavenging activity of CDs to O2 •− and H2O2. The structural characterization of CDs in Fig. 1 revealed the presence of electron-donor molecular groups, like -OH, -COOH, and -NH2, as well as an expandable sp2 hybrid carbon network structure. These features are responsible for the effective scavenging activity of ROS, ascribed to the hydrogen donor behavior [48]. The electron transfer promoted by the CDs and the availability of highly active sites contribute to the formation of adducts, such as O2 •− and H2O2, enhancing the ROS scavenging activity [49–51].

Both O2 •− and H2O2 have the potential damage on cell structure and function. The scavenging of both O2 •− and downstream product, H2O2, play a crucial role in maintaining redox homeostasis with organism [52,53]. It is widely known that SOD catalyzes the conversion of O2 •− into O2 and H2O2, while CAT decomposes H2O2 into H2O and O2. Therefore, the observed scavenging abilities of CDs towards O2 •− and H2O2 can be attributed to their stable and sustainable catalytic effects of SOD-like and CAT-like activities, respectively.

The ROS-scavenging activities of CDs were assessed in oxidative HUVECs to understand their impact on cellular oxidative stress. Initially, the biocompatibility of CDs was evaluated in both normal and oxidative cells. The CCK-8 assay revealed that H2O2 exhibited concentration-dependent cytotoxicity in HUVECs, with an estimated half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of approximately 500 μM (Fig. 3 A). Hence, a concentration of 500 μM H2O2 was chosen for subsequent experiments to induce oxidative stress injury in HUVECs. Meanwhile, there was no apparent cytotoxicity when HUVECs treated with CDs at the concentration range of 5 μg/mL to 50 μg/mL for 24 h compared with the control group (HUVECs without any intervention) (P = 0.2474) (Fig. 3B). When exposed to H2O2-induced oxidative stress injury in HUVECs for 24 h, cell viability improved with increasing concentrations of CDs (Fig. 3C). Similar results and trends were observed when CDs were dissolved in DMEM medium for 1 and 3 months (Fig. S3). Live/dead staining further confirmed that CDs exhibited no apparent cytotoxicity under normal conditions (Fig. S4). Moreover, in the presence of CDs, the level of dead cells in the oxidative stress condition was significantly reduced in a concentration-dependent manner, consistent with the results of the CCK-8 assay (Fig. 3D). These outcomes demonstrated the excellent biocompatibility of CDs and their catalytic ability to effectively alleviate oxidative stress, thereby protecting and restoring cells from ROS-induced cytotoxicity. Considering the antioxidant performance of CDs (Fig. 2) and the cytotoxicity evaluation, the low, medium, and high concentrations of CDs (5, 20 and 40 μg/mL) were chosen for subsequent examinations of the antioxidant capacity and angiogenesis in oxidative HUVECs.

To evaluate the alteration of ROS level in cells treated with or without CDs, DCFH-DA was applied as an indicator. As shown in Fig. 3E, strong green fluorescence signals were observed in oxidative HUVECs group (H2O2-treated HUVECs) compared to the control group (HUVECs without any intervention), indicating a high level of ROS in oxidative cells. However, when treated with increasing concentrations of CDs, the intracellular fluorescence signals remarkably decreased in a concentration-dependent manner. Quantitative analysis revealed a gradual reduction in intracellular ROS levels in 5 μg/mL CDs group (H2O2-treated HUVECs cocultured with 5 μg/mL CDs) (15.99 ± 0.69, P < 0.0001), 20 μg/mL CDs group (H2O2-treated HUVECs cocultured with 20 μg/mL CDs) (12.07 ± 0.47, P < 0.0001), and 40 μg/mL CDs group (H2O2-treated HUVECs cocultured with 40 μg/mL CDs) (4.39 ± 0.35, P < 0.0001) compared to the oxidative HUVECs group (21.54 ± 0.72) (Fig. 3F). Moreover, the level of MDA, a product of lipid oxidation, was assessed to reflect intracellular damage caused by oxidative stress. The MDA content in oxidized HUVECs seeded on CDs at concentration of 20 μg/mL (2.75 ± 0.27 μmol/mgprot, P < 0.0001) and 40 μg/mL (0.86 ± 0.16 μmol/mgprot, P < 0.0001) were significantly lower than that of oxidative HUVECs (Fig. 3G). These results indicated that the MDA content decreased with increasing concentrations of CDs in the oxidative stress microenvironment.

Efficient clearance O2 •−, H2O2, and free radical within cells through the activity of SOD and CAT plays a crucial role in the timely repair of cells damaged by oxidative stress. To further verify the catalytic capacity of CDs within cells, the SOD and CAT activities in oxidative HUVECs were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3H, compared to the oxidative HUVECs group, elevated levels of SOD activities were observed in oxidative HUVECs treated with different concentrations of CDs (P = 0.0003, P < 0.0001, P < 0.0001, respectively). Notably, the concentrations of 20 μg/mL and 40 μg/mL CDs significantly increased SOD activity compared to normal cells (P = 0.0011, P < 0.0001, respectively). In the CAT activity assay, the oxidative HUVECs group demonstrated the lowest CAT activity, almost approaching negligible levels (0.12 ± 0.01 U/mgprot) (Fig. 3I). However, treatment with CDs led to a notable increase in CAT activity levels compared to the oxidative HUVECs group. At a concentration of 40 μg/mL, the CAT activity reached levels comparable to those observed in normal HUVECs (106.13 ± 1.80 U/mgprot, P = 0.8523).

Besides, the expression of antioxidant-related genes, superoxide dismutase 2 (SOD2) and CAT was evaluated using RT-PCR. As expected, the mRNA levels of SOD2 and CAT exhibited a gradual upregulation with increasing concentrations of CDs (Fig. 3J and K). Nuclear factor (erythroid-derived 2)-like 2 (Nrf2) serves as a key regulator of cellular antioxidant processes, as it controls the transcription of antioxidant genes containing SOD and CAT [54]. To explore the involvement of Nrf2 in the antioxidant effects of CDs, the protein expression level of Nrf2 was examined using western blot analysis. The results demonstrated that Nrf2 expression was significantly lower in the oxidative HUVECs group compared to the control group. However, intervention with CDs led to an upregulation of Nrf2 expression (Fig. 3L, Fig. S5). All these above results illustrated that CDs could effectively scavenge the overproduction of ROS, providing protection and facilitating repair against oxidative damage through their catalytic activities of the mimic of SOD and CAT, possibly via the regulation of Nrf2 expression level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs attenuates H2O2-induced endothelia dysfunction in HUVECs
ce:section.ce:para: Persistent hyperglycemia leads to the overproduction of ROS, a phenomenon known to induce endothelia dysfunction and impaired angiogenesis in diabetes [55]. The migration of HUVECs serves as a pivotal biomarker for endothelia function and represents a crucial step in neovascularization [56]. A scratch wound test was employed to determine the impact of CDs on HUVECs migration under oxidative stress conditions (Fig. 4 A). Phase contrast images were captured at 12-h intervals up to 24 h, and the remaining cell-free area was quantitated at the different time points to evaluate the rate of cell migration (Fig. 4B and C). The results showed that at 24 h, cell migration in the oxidative HUVECs group was notably impaired compared to the control group (P = 0.0147), whereas no significant difference was observed at 12 h (P = 0.1579). Notably, the presence of CDs promoted cell migration under oxidative stress at both 12 and 24 h. At the final observation point, the wound closure rates for low, medium, and high concentrations of CDs were 67.09 ± 3.89% (P = 0.0432 vs control), 71.61 ± 7.56% (P = 0.0054 vs control), and 99.64 ± 0.46% (P < 0.0001 vs control), respectively. These results indicated that CDs facilitated cell migration under ROS conditions, promoting wound closure and suggesting a potential beneficial effect on angiogenesis in an oxidative environment.

The impact of CDs on H2O2-induced apoptosis in HUVECs was examined using flow cytometry assay and TUNEL staining. The oxidative HUVECs group exhibited a significant increase in apoptosis compared to the control group (P < 0.0001). However, the presence of CDs in a dose-dependent manner reduced H2O2-induced apoptosis in HUVECs (Fig. 4D). Treatment with CDs resulted in a decrease in the proportion of apoptosis induced by H2O2, from 27.72 ± 1.82 to 5.78 ± 0.58%, with statistically differences (Fig. 4E). Similarly, TUNEL staining and quantification showed that CDs treatment decreased the average number of apoptotic cells from 20.33 ± 1.53 cells/fields to 5.27 ± 0.26 cells/fields (Fig. S6). The findings indicated that CDs could alleviate H2O2-induced apoptosis in HUVECs.

Mitochondria is the site which generates most of the ROS in endothelia [57,58]. Increased ROS in mitochondria reduce membrane potential. JC-1 staining was performed to examine the changed mitochondria membrane potentials of H2O2-treated HUVECs with or without CDs treatment. As shown in Figs. S7A and a significantly higher percentage of green fluorescence intensity (JC-1 monomer form) illustrated lower mitochondria membrane potential was observed in H2O2-treated HUVECs, suggesting the increased ROS production in mitochondria. The treatment of CDs increased the red fluorescence intensity (JC-1 aggregate form) with reduced green fluorescence intensity, representing the recovery of mitochondria membrane potential ascribed to decreased ROS (Fig. S7B). Above results indicated that CDs could alleviate mitochondria dysfunction caused by excessive ROS production.

The in vitro tube formation assay was operated to simulate the reorganization stage of angiogenesis (Fig. 4F). Based on the captured images and quantitative analysis, there was nearly no tube formation observed in the oxidative HUVECs group. Under H2O2-induced oxidative stress, the average number of branch points in the three groups treated with different concentrations of CDs (5 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, and 40 μg/mL) increased from 1.71 ± 0.44 to 16.83 ± 1.31, while the average length of tubes raised from 7.37 ± 1.11 to 27.25 ± 1.05. A significant increasing trend in tube formation was observed with the elevation of CDs concentration compared to the oxidative HUVECs group (Fig. 4G and H). Furthermore, an ELISA assay was performed to detect the VEGFA released by HUVECs in order to assess the angiogenesis capability of the CDs under oxidative stress. Consistent with the results of the tube formation assay, the oxidative HUVECs group exhibited a lower secretion of VEGFA compared to the control group (P = 0.0108). When the oxidized HUVECs were cultured with CDs, there was a significant increase in the VEGFA level compared to the oxidative HUVECs group. Moreover, the secretion of VEGFA in the medium and high concentrations of CDs (20 and 40 μg/mL) surpassed that of the control group (Fig. S8A). The Western blot revealed VEGFA expression was significant lower in oxidative HUVECs group compared to the control group, while it was upregulated by the intervention of CDs (Fig. 4I, Fig. S8B). In addition, the mRNA expression level of VEGFA was assessed by RT-PCR. As shown in Fig. 4J, oxidative HUVECs group exhibited the lowest expression of VEGFA. Remarkably, the significant enhanced expression observed in 20 and 40 μg/mL CDs group compared to oxidative HUVECs group (P = 0.0001 and P < 0.0001, respectively). Taken together, CDs have the potential to mitigate oxidative-induced cellular damage and restore endothelial function by scavenging ROS, thus providing a foundation for promoting vascularization in diabetes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The molecular mechanism of promoting angiogenesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the underlying molecular mechanism of CDs, transcriptome sequencing was first performed on oxidative HUVECs without and with treating CDs at a concentration of 40 μg/mL. A total of 1896 different expressed genes (DEGs) were identified, consisting of 896 upregulated genes and 1000 downregulated genes (Fig. 5 A). Heatmaps were generated to visually represent genes with log2 (fold change) values exceeding 2 (Fig. 5B). Among these DEGs, several play crucial roles in maintaining redox balance, such as IDH1 [59], SOD2 [60], GREB1 [61], MSMO1 [62], and IDI1 [63]. Moreover, some of these genes have been proven to be related to angiogenesis, such as LPIN1 [64], PTK6 [65] and CDA [66]. Notably, compared to the oxidative HUVECs group, treatment with CDs led to an increase in the expression levels of antioxidant-related genes (LSS, STARD4, FDFT1, GUCY1A2, SOD2, GREB1, MOB3B, IDI1, TMCC2, MSMO1, IDH1, PPP2R2C) and angiogenesis-related genes (HMGCS1, LPIN1, PTK6, CDA), while downregulating pro-oxidant genes (HSPA6 and CDH10). The Gene Ontology (GO) annotation classification analysis revealed that the DEGs were associated with biosynthesis and metabolism of sterol/cholesterol in the biological process ontology (Fig. 5C). Cholesterol is an essential component of biological membranes and plays a crucial role in maintaining membrane integrity and regulating signaling pathways [67]. Depletion of cholesterol in the membrane can disrupt multiple signaling processes related to cell adhesion, migration, survival, and proliferation. Recent research has highlighted the significant role of cholesterol in regulating angiogenesis [68]. Additionally, endothelial functions-related GO terms such as “cell-substrate junction”, “focal adhesion” and “GTPase activity” were identified in the cellular component ontology and the molecular function ontology. These findings suggest that the response to oxidative stress and modulation of endothelial function are closely associated with the therapeutic mechanisms underlying CDs treatment.

Moreover, Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway analysis indicated that the treatment of CDs resulted in the activation of several signaling pathways, including endocytosis, PI3K-AKT signaling pathway, and MAPK signaling pathway (Fig. 5D). Among these DEGs, 143 genes were shared with the endocytosis-related pathway, 51 genes were shared with the PI3K-related pathway, and 44 genes were shared with the MAPK-related pathway (Fig. S9). The WB results illustrated a significant reduction of phosphorylated PI3K (p-PI3K) and phosphorylated AKT (p-AKT) in oxidative HUVECs group compared with the control group (P < 0.0001, P < 0.0001, respectively). And CDs could significantly increase the levels of p-PI3K and p-AKT in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 6 A–C). The results suggested that the significant potential role of CDs regulating oxidant-antioxidant balance and angiogenesis possibly via the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway.

To further validate the involvement of PI3K-AKT signaling pathway in the effects of CDs treatment, we utilized the PI3K inhibitor LY294002 and activator 740 Y-P. When 20 μg/mL CDs were cultured with 30 μM LY294002 under H2O2-induced ROS environment, the levels of p-PI3K and p-AKT were significant downregulated compared to the treatment of 20 μg/mL CDs alone (P = 0.0083, P = 0.0042, respectively) (Fig. 6D–F). Meanwhile, the increase in Nrf2 expression induced by CDs treatment was disappeared in the presence of LY294002 (Fig. S10). Subsequently, we examined the catalytic activity of CDs of the mimic enzymes of SOD and CAT. The SOD and CAT activities were reduced, and the MDA content level was increased in the group treated with 20 μg/mL CDs + LY294002 compared to the group treated with 20 μg/mL CDs alone (Fig. S11). In contrast, when 20 μg/mL CDs were cultured with 30 μM 740 Y-P under a simulated oxidative stress environment, the levels of p-PI3K, p-AKT, and Nrf2 were obviously upregulated compared to the treatment with CDs alone (P = 0.0022, P = 0.0038 and P = 0.0832, respectively) (Fig. 6G–I, Fig. S12). Equally, the SOD and CAT activities were increased, while the level of MDA content was decreased in the group treated with 20 μg/mL CDs + 740 Y-P compared to the group treated with 20 μg/mL CDs alone (Fig. S13). These results indicated that CDs response to oxidative stress through the PI3K/AKT/Nrf2 pathway. In addition, the expression level of VEGFA in Western blot and ELISA were remarkably decreased upon CDs + LY294002 group than that with CDs alone (Fig. 6D, Fig. S14), while the expression of VEGFA was increased with the 740 Y-P intervention (Fig. 6G, Fig. S15). These results indicated that CDs promote angiogenesis through the PI3K/AKT/VEGFA pathway. In summary, the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway is involved in the antioxidant and angiogenesis effects of CDs on HUVECs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The in vivo angiogenic effect of CDs in diabetic rat
ce:section.ce:para: The impaired angiogenesis at implantation sites, a common issue in diabetes, significantly hinders effective implant osseointegration. To assess the in vivo angiogenic effect of CDs in ROS-induced injury, we established a subcutaneous implantation model in diabetic SD rat (Fig. 7 A). Firstly, the progressive weight loss and elevated blood glucose level above 16.7 mmol/L confirmed the successful induction of the diabetic rat model (Fig. 7B and C) [69]. Next, we evaluated the SOD and CAT activity, as well as MDA content in tissues surrounding the implants in the NC, PC, CP5, CP20, and CP40 groups. The SOD activity was significantly reduced in the PC group compared to the NC group (2.75 ± 1.02 U/mgprot vs 6.04 ± 1.12 U/mgprot, P = 0.0421) (Fig. S16A). Similar results were obtained in CAT activity evaluation (0.25 ± 0.02 U/mgprot vs 112.25 ± 23.17 U/mgprot, P < 0.0001) (Fig. S16B). Additionally, the PC group exhibited a higher MDA content compared to the NC group (7.82 ± 2.37 μmol/mgprot vs 3.52 ± 1.26 μmol/mgprot, P < 0.0001) (Fig. S16C). Above results implied that the diabetic rats experienced higher oxidative stress levels compared to normal SD rats. However, with CDs treatment, the activity of SOD and CAT increased, while the MDA content decreased. After 7 days of implantation, fewer blood vessels were observed in the PC group compared to the NC group. Macroscopic observation of subcutaneous tissue surrounding the implants revealed an increased presence of blood vessels with enhanced vasculature as the concentration of CDs increased (Fig. 7D). H&E staining demonstrated no bacterial infection in the five groups. Neutrophil infiltration (indicated by black arrow) was most pronounced in the PC group, followed by the NC, CP5, CP20, and CP40 groups, suggesting a higher incidence of inflammatory reactions associated with implantation in diabetes mellitus. However, CDs treatment was able to alleviate local inflammation while scavenging oxidative stress around the implant. The angiogenetic markers of CD31 and von Willebrand Factor (vWF), as well as antioxidant marker of Nrf2 were investigated to evaluate the induced neovascularization and antioxidant ability of CDs (Fig. 7E). Immunohistochemistry staining revealed the lowest expression level of CD31 and vWF (18.72 ± 3.27%, 17.82 ± 2.93%, respectively). Nevertheless, the CP20 and CP40 group showed higher expression levels of CD31 (31.72 ± 14.29%, P = 0.0120; 41.89 ± 15.63%, P < 0.0001, respectively) and vWF (36.14 ± 12.29%, P = 0.0001; 52.17 ± 16.24%, P < 0.0001, respectively) compared to the NC group (Fig. 7F and G). In addition, Nrf2 expression in CDs-loaded groups were significantly higher than that in PC group (P = 0.0158, P < 0.0001, P < 0.0001, respectively) (Fig. 7H). The pathological sections stained by H&E (Fig. S17) showed that the tissue structure of the organs in the different groups were not significantly different from that in the negative control group, implying favorable biocompatibility of CDs in vivo. These results further confirm that CDs can enhance the angiogenic response of diabetic peri-implantation tissues by maintaining the balance between oxidation and antioxidants.

Tissue recover is often delayed ascribed to the poor angiogenesis ability in diabetes [70,71]. Using the wound healing as model, the promotion effect of CDs on vascularization was further evaluated using a diabetic mouse model (Fig. 8 A). Same to prior subcutaneous implantation model, the weight and blood glucose were monitored to confirmed diabetic rats established (Fig. 8B and C). The SOD, CAT activity and MDA content of wound tissue area were tested to evaluate the changed oxidative stress in the five groups (Fig. S18). PC group showed the lowest activity of SOD and CAT compared to NC group (3.18 ± 1.12 U/mgprot vs 6.92 ± 1.58 U/mgprot, P = 0.0053; 0.35 ± 0.04 U/mgprot vs 121.38 ± 23.31 U/mgprot, P < 0.0001, respectively), while showed the highest content of MDA (7.88 ± 3.41 μmol/mgprot vs 3.79 ± 1.26 μmol/mgprot, P < 0.0001). With the intervention of CDs, the SOD and CAT activity of experiment groups raised from 7.02 ± 2.21 U/mgprot to 12.35 ± 2.97 U/mgprot, from 73.38 ± 26.31 U/mgprot to 102.77 ± 12.37 U/mgprot, respectively. Meanwhile, the MDA content decreased from 3.56 ± 1.82 μmol/mgprot to 1.52 ± 0.87 μmol/mgprot. The representative photographs of the diabetic wounds from five groups at 0 d, 3 d, 7 d, 10 d and 14 d after surgery were displayed intuitively in Fig. 8D. The results showed that the wound area of NC (0.75 ± 0.23), C20 (1.63 ± 0.49) and C40 (0.27 ± 0.06) were lower than the PC (52.78 ± 0.66) and C5 (24.93 ± 1.68) groups at day 14, suggesting higher concentration of CDs could accelerate wound healing in diabetic rats (Fig. 8E). Excessive ROS accumulation is one of the most disturbing problems in diabetic wound healing, which leading to proteins degradation, impaired dermal fibroblast and keratinocyte function [72]. Thus, we detected antioxidant performance in five groups using Nrf2 as an indicator. The expression level of Nrf2 in C20 and C40 groups were higher than other three groups at day 7 (35.13 ± 14.57%, 46.21 ± 17.25%, respectively) and 14 (43.17 ± 19.21%, 57.79 ± 18.26%, respectively), while NC and C5 groups showed no significant difference (P = 0.1985, P = 0.2214, respectively) (Fig. 9 C). H&E and Masson trichrome staining were performed to evaluate the efficiency of dermal layer formation (Fig. 9A). Proper collagen deposition and remodeling improves the tensile strength of the tissue and produces better therapeutic effect in wound healing [73]. The H&E-stained wound sections revealed that the wounds of the NC, C20, and C40 groups had more granulation tissue at day 7 and 14 post-injury. Masson collagen staining showed that NC, C20, and C40 groups had more collagen deposition compared with that in PC and C5 groups at day 7 and 14. Type I collagen (Col-I) is the main component of dermal ECM and play a very important role in wound healing [74]. Immunohistochemical of collagen deposition marker Col-I revealed same results with histology staining (Fig. 9B). C20 and C40 groups behaved more Col-I positive area compared to other three groups at day 7 (37.26 ± 11.23%, 51.27 ± 18.45%, respectively) and 14 (43.75 ± 12.38%, 61.43 ± 19.26%, respectively) (Fig. 9D). Blood vessels were supposed to support the transportation of oxygen and nutrients, and the regeneration of skin appendages. Accelerated angiogenesis has a positive effect on the wound healing and even reconstruction of skin function in diabetic wounds [75]. Hence, the angiogenesis of wound sites was further identified by immunohistochemical staining of CD31 and vWF. As shown in Fig. 9E, the expression of CD31 in C5, C20, and C40 groups were higher than that in NC groups at day 7 (P = 0.0012, P < 0.0001, P < 0.0001, respectively) and 14 (P = 0.0008, P < 0.0001, P < 0.0001, respectively). The expression of vWF in C20 and C40 groups were higher than that in NC, PC, and C5 groups at day 7 (37.12 ± 15.07%, 47.26 ± 10.27%, respectively) and 14 (43.15 ± 11.29%, 60.07 ± 16.28%, respectively), while there was no significant difference between NC and C5 groups (P = 0.2257, P = 0.2168, respectively) (Fig. 9F). The pathological sections stained by H&E show that the tissue structure of the organs in the different groups with variable doses of CDs were not significantly different from that in the NC group, implying that CDs do not produce pathological damage to the five major organs of diabetic rats (Fig. S19). Overall, these results demonstrated the feasibility of using antioxidant CDs for the treatment of diabetic wounds. They effectively alleviate oxidative stress at wound sites, facilitate fibroblast activity, collagen deposition, and angiogenesis.

Impaired angiogenesis is a prominent feature observed in patients with diabetes [76,77]. Research has shown that the accumulation of ROS caused by high glucose can lead to dysfunction of endothelial cells, further exacerbating angiogenesis complications in diabetic patients [78,79]. In such a microenvironment, an unsatisfactory repairing process poses significant challenges for orthopedic patients with diabetes. Prolonged treatment periods and poor prognosis not only bring financial burdens but also cause physical and mental stress. Consequently, strategies aimed at promoting angiogenesis and improving clinical outcomes for orthopedic patients with diabetes are of paramount importance. Nanomedicines offer new possibilities for such treatments, as they can be utilized to load bioactive molecules or drugs, or leverage their specific structural characteristics to provide binding sites for catalytic reactions in the body [80,81]. Nevertheless, traditional nanomaterials suffer from limitations such as low loading efficiency, and potential toxicity [82]. To date, limited research has been focused on the promotion of angiogenesis through the alleviation of excessive oxidative stress by nanocatalytic drugs. Although studies have evaluated the antioxidative and angiogenesis activities of nanomaterials, a comprehensive molecular mechanism explanation has been lacking. More importantly, insufficient research has been conducted to evaluate the efficacy of nanomedicines for vascularization in diabetes. Correspondingly, in this study, we designed and synthesized carbon dots (CDs) to effectively promote angiogenesis by scavenging excessive ROS. In addition to evaluating the performance of CDs in vitro and in vivo, we provided reasonable mechanism explanation.

CDs with specific biological functions could be synthesized by introducing appropriate raw materials and controlling reaction conditions [83–85]. In this study, CDs with the ability to promote angiogenesis were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Characterization results demonstrated that CDs had a size of 2.8 nm and exhibited fluorescent properties. The molecular structure of the CDs included amido, hydroxide, and carboxyl groups (Fig. 1), which are significant for their function [81]. CDs exhibited favorable free radical scavenging efficiency in eliminating ROS (Table S2). This indicated that the CDs possessed both SOD and CAT activity, enabling them to catalytically scavenge O2 •− and H2O2 (Fig. 2). Moreover, the CDs increased cellular SOD and CAT activity under oxidative stress conditions, thus scavenging O2 •− and H2O2 more efficiently in vitro (Fig. 3H and I). The scratch wound test confirmed that the CDs promoted endotheliocyte migration (Fig. 4A). In the tube formation assay, the CDs facilitated angiogenesis by upregulating the expression of VEGFA under oxidative stress, as validated by ELISA and RT-PCR (Fig. 4F–H, J, Fig. S8). The stability of nanocatalytic medicine is a crucial concern. After one month and three months, the EC50 of CDs to ABTS•+ radicals were approximately 8.08 μg/mL and 9.18 μg/mL, respectively, suggesting the long-term stability of the scavenging efficiency (Fig. S1). Compared with EC50 of AA (3.33 μg/mL and 4.58 μg/mL), it still exhibited the well capacity. Favorable biocompatibility of CDs was also found after one month and three months of storage using CCK-8 methods (Fig. S3). The preferable angiogenesis performance of the CDs in oxidative damaged cells in vitro inspired further investigation of the molecular mechanism. RNA-seq analysis revealed that the therapeutic mechanisms of CDs treatment involved the response to oxidative stress and promotion of angiogenesis through the regulation of the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway (Fig. 5). Western blot revealed that CDs enhanced the levels of p-PI3K and p-AKT under oxidative stress and upregulated the expression level of Nrf2 and VEGFA (Figs. 3L, 4I and 6A). Meanwhile, the intervention of PI3K inhibitor and activator modulated the expression levels of Nrf2, VEGFA and the phosphorylation levels of PI3K and AKT accordingly (Fig. 6D, G). Interestingly, GO enrichment analysis showed that the therapeutic effect of CDs may affect angiogenesis by regulating sterol/cholesterol metabolism, but the specific molecular mechanism needs to be further studied. Considering the challenges of orthopedic diseases in diabetes, using subcutaneous implantation and wound healing in diabetic rat as the models of orthopedic diseases, in vivo evaluations were performed and illustrated the promotion of angiogenesis on the implant and the treated tissue for repairing (Figs. 7, Figs. 8 and 9). Furthermore, the well biological safety of CDs on the main organ of model rats implies the acceptable clinical application possibility in diabetes.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized CDs using a one-step hydrothermal method. These CDs demonstrate a remarkable capacity to promote angiogenesis, offering a promising approach for treating orthopedic diseases with diabetic mellitus. Notably, the CDs exhibit potent catalytic activity, acting as both SOD and CAT to efficiently scavenging O2 •− and H2O2. Moreover, the CDs effectively restored endothelial function and facilitated angiogenesis in vitro. Transcriptome sequencing and molecular biological experiments revealed that the CDs activated the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway, a crucial contributing to augmented angiogenesis under simulated oxidative condition characteristic of diabetes. Furthermore, our in vivo experiments in diabetic rat models have demonstrated the CDs' favorable capacity for neovascularization, promising significant advancements in the treatment of diabetes-related orthopedic diseases. These findings provide a valuable foundation of nanocatalytic drugs like carbon dots for the promising clinical applications in the treatment of diabetes through promoting angiogenesis.
